text:
Day 9 of the Olympics is here, and it芒聙聶s another big one. Here芒聙聶s my daily guide to help you know what events to watch. It should do the trick whether you芒聙聶re planning to spend a full Sunday in front of the TV or just picking and choosing spots.

You can also subscribe toSports Illustrated芒聙聶s Daily Rings, my daily podcast where Dan Gartland and I are recapping the Games every night. New episodes have been published every night before NBC芒聙聶s prime-time coverage is even over.

Here芒聙聶s what I芒聙聶ll be watching Sunday, August 4.

This is not a comprehensive list;visit Olympics.comfor a full schedule. All events will stream live on Peacock. I芒聙聶ll also be tweeting out a schedule of medal events, Team USA games and other favorites each day.

My Day 9 Olympics viewing guide! Hopefully this time with no typos, but no promises. Fun Sunday on tap#Paris2024pic.twitter.com/5G2UxDMYRQ

It芒聙聶s time for the men芒聙聶s 100 meters. Team USA芒聙聶s Noah Lyles is the reigning world champ, though Jamaica芒聙聶s Kishane Thompson is the betting favorite. No American man has won this event since 2004芒聙聰of course a guy by the name of Usain Bolt was in the way for a while.

The race starts at 3:50 p.m. ET, and don芒聙聶t tune in late because it芒聙聶ll be over in less than 10 seconds. The other gold medals that芒聙聶ll be awarded during the afternoon track and field session are the men芒聙聶s hammer throw and women芒聙聶s high jump.

Sunday is the final round of the men芒聙聶s golf tournament, and we芒聙聶ve got a great leaderboard. American Xander Schauffele is tied with Spain芒聙聶s John Rahm for first place at 14-under. Then Great Britain芒聙聶s Tommy Fleetwood is one shot behind. They芒聙聶ll make up the final group. American and world No. 1 Scottie Scheffler is in a group four shots off the lead.

Golf begins at 3 a.m. ET, but the leaders won芒聙聶t go off until 6:39 a.m. and should hit the back nine in the nine o芒聙聶clock hour.

It芒聙聶s the last day of tennis, and we芒聙聶ve got three Olympic matches left. We芒聙聶ll see bronze and gold matches to wrap up women芒聙聶s doubles, with a dream matchup in between them.脗

The men芒聙聶s singles tournament has come down to Novak Djokovic vs. Carlos Alcaraz, the top two seeds in the tournament. Djokovic has an all-time record 24 Grand Slam singles titles; he芒聙聶s won everything there is to win except an Olympic gold. Alcaraz has won four Grand Slams and is viewed as the next man up.

Tennis starts at 6 a.m. and they芒聙聶ll be the second match on the court, followed by the women芒聙聶s doubles gold match.

The final day of indoor swimming (we still have marathon swimming to come) has four medal events beginning at 12:30 p.m. ET: the women芒聙聶s 50-meter freestyle, the men芒聙聶s 1500-meter free, and both the men芒聙聶s and women芒聙聶s 4X100 medley relays.

In cycling, the men芒聙聶s road race was a fun watch Saturday morning and the women芒聙聶s event goes off Sunday at 8 a.m. ET.

Beach volleyball has reached the round of 16. Team USA芒聙聶s Sara Hughes and Kelly Cheng will face a team from Italy at 4 p.m. ET. (The Nuss/Kloth team will have its round of 16 game the next day.)

If you read my post yesterday, you know how excited I am forkayak cross: Heats continue on Sunday, now with four kayaks in the water at once instead of three. Team USA芒聙聶s Evy Leibfarth will be in the fifth heat of the session at 10:45 a.m. ET.

Team USA archer Brady Ellison already won his fourth Olympic medal this week in the mixed team event. He has an early-morning quarterfinal in the individual competition, hoping to advance all the way to the medal matches just after 8:30 a.m. ET.

The U.S. women芒聙聶s basketball team continues group play against Germany at 11:15 a.m. ET.

The men芒聙聶s singles table tennis medals will be awarded Sunday morning. Defending Olympic silver medalist Fan Zhendong will go for gold andfan-favorite 17-year-old Felix LeBrunwill compete for bronze. Action starts at 7:30 a.m. ET.

Finally, listeners of my podcast know I芒聙聶ll be glued to the Croatia vs. Spain men芒聙聶s handball game at 3 p.m. ET. Only one will advance out of group play.

Mitch Goldich is a senior editor for Sports Illustrated, mostly focused on the NFL. He has also covered the Olympics extensively and written on a variety of sports since joining SI in 2014. His work has been published by The New York Times, Baseball Prospectus and Food & Wine, among other outlets. Goldich has a bachelor芒聙聶s in journalism from Lehigh University and a master芒聙聶s in journalism from the Medill School at Northwestern University. He is a Philly native, now living in Boston, and he invented and popularized the octopus, when the same player scores a touchdown and the ensuing two-point conversion.

title: Noah Lyles Goes for First Gold to Headline Sunday芒聙聶s Packed Olympic Slate
description: The American sprinter seeks to earn the title of world芒聙聶s fastest man in the 100-meter dash. Plus, tennis and men芒聙聶s golf comes to a close.
